_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_

**Honor the requested branch during imports.**

`repoBranch`/`branch` is computed in both import paths, but these helpers never send a `ref` to the Contents API. Imports from a non-default branch will still walk the default branch and return the wrong files.

<details>
<summary>Patch sketch</summary>

```diff
 async function fetchRepoContents(
   owner: string,
   repo: string,
   repoPath: string = '',
+  branch?: string,
   token?: string
 ): Promise<GitHubFile[]> {
-  const url = `${GITHUB_API}/repos/${owner}/${repo}/contents/${repoPath}`;
-  const response = await fetchGitHubApi(url, token);
+  const url = new URL(`${GITHUB_API}/repos/${owner}/${repo}/contents/${repoPath}`);
+  if (branch) url.searchParams.set('ref', branch);
+  const response = await fetchGitHubApi(url.toString(), token);
   ...
 }
```

Thread `branch` through `recursivelyFetchDirectory()` and pass `repoBranch`/`branch` from both POST import branches.
</details>


Also applies to: 101-124, 567-578, 625-636

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In `@app/api/integrations/github/route.ts` around lines 75 - 89, fetchRepoContents
currently ignores the requested branch/ref so imports always use the default
branch; update fetchRepoContents(owner, repo, repoPath = '', token?, branch?) to
accept a branch/ref and include it when calling the Contents API (either append
?ref=<branch> to the URL or pass as option to fetchGitHubApi), return JSON as
before, and propagate that branch argument through
recursivelyFetchDirectory(...) and the two POST import flows so that both import
paths pass repoBranch/branch into recursivelyFetchDirectory and ultimately into
fetchRepoContents.
```

</details>

_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_

**Validate `maxFiles` before using it as the crawl budget.**

Both POST branches trust `body.maxFiles` directly. Non-numeric values turn the `existingFiles.size >= maxFiles` checks into `false`, and oversized values let one request buffer a very large repo in memory.

<details>
<summary>Patch sketch</summary>

```diff
-      const { owner, repo, branch, maxFiles = 50 } = body;
+      const { owner, repo, branch } = body;
+      const maxFiles = z.coerce.number().int().min(1).max(500).catch(50).parse(body.maxFiles);
```

Apply the same coercion/clamp in the legacy `{ url, maxFiles }` branch.
</details>

_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_

<details>
<summary>🧩 Analysis chain</summary>

🌐 Web query:

`LinkedIn API v2 shares endpoint visibility format`

💡 Result:

The LinkedIn API v2 "shares" endpoint refers to the legacy /v2/shares API (marked as deprecated in current docs), used for creating and retrieving shares via POST/GET https://api.linkedin.com/v2/shares. Visibility is specified in the response under distribution.linkedInDistributionTarget.visibleToGuest (boolean: true for public/guest visible, false otherwise). Example from docs: {"distribution":{"linkedInDistributionTarget":{"visibleToGuest":true}}}. No explicit "format" for input visibility in create requests; it's handled via distribution targeting. However, current primary endpoint for creating shares/posts is /v2/ugcPosts (UGC Posts API, recommended over legacy shares). Visibility format: {"com.linkedin.ugc.MemberNetworkVisibility": "PUBLIC"} or "CONNECTIONS". **Anonymous users get a new filesystem ID per request—session continuity is lost.**

When an unauthenticated user makes the first request, `resolveRequestAuth()` returns `success: false` (no cookie exists yet). The fallback at line 251 generates a new random ID via `generateSecureId()`, but this ID is never persisted to the `anon-session-id` cookie. Subsequent requests will also find no cookie, causing another new ID to be generated. Each request operates in an isolated, ephemeral workspace.

The codebase already has proper session persistence handling in `lib/virtual-filesystem/resolve-filesystem-owner.ts` (which stores the ID in an HttpOnly cookie via `setAnonSessionCookie()`), but the chat route uses inline logic that skips this critical step. Either use `resolveFilesystemOwner()` instead of the inline logic, or ensure the generated ID is persisted to the cookie before returning it.

🌐 Web query:

`jsonwebtoken library verify signature error type JsonWebTokenError InvalidSignatureError`

💡 Result:

In the jsonwebtoken library, when jwt.verify fails due to a signature mismatch, it throws a JsonWebTokenError with the message "invalid signature". JsonWebTokenError is the base error class for general JWT verification failures (e.g., invalid token format, missing signature, algorithm mismatch, invalid audience/issuer). InvalidSignatureError is not a distinct error type in this library; the specific "invalid signature" case uses JsonWebTokenError. From the source code in verify.js: if (!valid) { return done(new JsonWebTokenError('invalid signature')); } Official documentation lists under JsonWebTokenError possible messages including 'invalid signature'. Common causes: - Secret/key mismatch between sign and verify - Incorrect key format (e.g., PEM for RS256 without proper formatting) - Algorithm mismatch (specify algorithms: ['HS256'] etc.) - Token tampering or corruption - Encoding issues with secrets

Citations:

- 1: https://www.npmjs.com/package/jsonwebtoken
- 2: https://github.com/auth0/node-jsonwebtoken/blob/da8f55c3c7b4dd0bfc07a2df228500fdd050242a/verify.js
- 3: https://npmdoc.github.io/node-npmdoc-jsonwebtoken/build/apidoc.html
- 4: https://github.com/auth0/node-jsonwebtoken/issues/768

---



</details>

**Orphaned checkpoints: `deleteSnapshot` does not clean up the underlying checkpoint.**

When a snapshot is deleted, only the in-memory metadata is removed. The actual checkpoint persists at the provider level, causing storage leaks. The same issue exists in `evictLRU()` (line 223).

However, the proposed fix cannot work as written:
- `CheckpointSystem.delete()` does not exist; CheckpointSystem only provides `create`, `restore`, `list`, and `branch` methods
- `deleteSnapshot(snapshotId)` receives only a snapshot ID and has no access to the `SandboxHandle` needed to call provider deletion methods
- `SnapshotManager` stores only `sourceSandboxId` (a string) but not the handle reference, making cleanup impossible at this layer

The underlying issue is real and requires architectural changes: either store handle references in snapshot metadata, provide a callback mechanism for cleanup, or move checkpoint deletion responsibility to callers of `deleteSnapshot()`.

The documentation states that the Edge Runtime does not support all Node.js APIs, including native Node.js APIs and globals, and lists only specific supported Web APIs and polyfills like process.env for environment variables. process.versions.node is a Node.js-specific property not listed in supported APIs. - 4: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/54739

🌐 Web query:

`Auth0 nextjs-auth0 getAccessTokenForConnection vs getAccessToken GitHub`

💡 Result:

In the `@auth0/nextjs-auth0` SDK (v4+), getAccessToken fetches an access token for the currently logged-in user from their Auth0 session. It supports optional audience and scope parameters (requiring Multi-Resource Refresh Tokens or MRRT for non-default audiences) and automatically refreshes expired tokens using the refresh token if available. It returns the token string by default or full response with { includeFullResponse: true }. Usable on server (via Auth0Client or helpers) and client (via /auth/access-token route). getAccessTokenForConnection({ connection: string, login_hint?: string }) is a newer method (renamed from getFederatedConnectionAccessToken in merged PR `#2010`, April 2025) for obtaining federated connection access tokens (e.g., Google OAuth2 token). It performs a token exchange via Auth0's /oauth/token endpoint using the user's refresh token as subject_token, with grant_type "urn:auth0:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange:federated-connection-access-token" and requested_token_type "http://auth0.com/oauth/token-type/federated-connection-access-token". Tokens are cached separately per connection to avoid cookie size limits. Requires a valid session with refresh token; throws MISSING_REFRESH_TOKEN if absent. Primarily server-side, similar to getAccessToken. Key differences: - Purpose: Standard app/API access token vs. upstream IdP (e.g., GitHub/Google) access token for federated connections. - Mechanism: Session token refresh/exchange vs. specialized token-exchange grant for connections. - Use case: Calling your APIs (with audience) vs. acting as the user in the original provider (e.g., GitHub API on user's behalf). - Caching: Shared session vs. per-connection cookies. Examples in repo EXAMPLES.md under "Getting access tokens for connections". Available post-PR merge (current as of 2026).

Citations:

- 1: https://auth0.github.io/nextjs-auth0/functions/client.getAccessToken.html
- 2: https://github.com/auth0/nextjs-auth0/blob/main/EXAMPLES.md
- 3: https://github.com/auth0/nextjs-auth0/pull/2010
- 4: https://github.com/auth0/nextjs-auth0

🌐 Web query:

`Auth0 nextjs-auth0 SDK getAccessTokenForConnection connection parameter`

💡 Result:

The getAccessTokenForConnection method in the Auth0 nextjs-auth0 SDK retrieves a federated connection access token (also known as FCAT) for a specific connection (e.g., 'google-oauth2') using the user's refresh token via Auth0's token exchange endpoint. Usage (server-side, e.g., App Router): import { auth0 } from "@/lib/auth0"; const tokenResponse = await auth0.getAccessTokenForConnection({ connection: "google-oauth2", // Required: name of the federated connection login_hint: "auth0|123456" // Optional: for specific user targeting }); This exchanges the refresh token for a connection-specific access token using: - grant_type: "urn:auth0:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange:federated-connection-access-token" - subject_token_type: "urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:refresh_token" - requested_token_type: "http://auth0.com/oauth/token-type/federated-connection-access-token" The connection parameter is required and specifies the federated/social connection (e.g., Google, GitHub). Tokens are stored statelessly in cookies (with browser size limits) or statefully in a session store. Requires a valid user session with refresh token; throws MISSING_REFRESH_TOKEN if not logged in. Added in PR `#2010` (merged April 2025), renamed from getFederatedConnectionAccessToken. See EXAMPLES.md for full usage.

Citations:

- 1: https://github.com/auth0/nextjs-auth0/blob/main/EXAMPLES.md
- 2: https://github.com/auth0/nextjs-auth0/pull/2010

🌐 Web query:

`Does FileSystemDirectoryHandle.getDirectoryHandle() accept a path with slashes as the name argument?`

💡 Result:

No, FileSystemDirectoryHandle.getDirectoryHandle does not accept a path with slashes as the name argument. The 'name' parameter is a string representing the name of a single subdirectory within the current directory handle, not a full path. To access nested directories, you must call the method iteratively, once per path segment. For example, to access "folder/subfolder": const subDir1 = await dirHandle.getDirectoryHandle('folder'); const subDir2 = await subDir1.getDirectoryHandle('subfolder'); Passing a slashed path like 'folder/subfolder' as 'name' will throw a TypeError, as slashes interfere with the native file system. This is confirmed by MDN documentation describing 'name' as "the FileSystemHandle.name of the subdirectory" (single name), with TypeError for invalid characters, and examples using simple names without slashes. The API design requires recursive calls for paths, unlike the deprecated DirectoryEntry API which accepted paths.

Citations:

- 1: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/FileSystemDirectoryHandle/getDirectoryHandle
- 2: https://developer.mozilla.org/ja/docs/Web/API/FileSystemDirectoryHandle/getDirectoryHandle
- 3: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/FileSystemDirectoryHandle
- 4: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/FileSystemDirectoryHandle/getFileHandle
- 5: https://wicg.github.io/file-system-access/
- 6: https://github.com/WICG/file-system-access/issues/437

---

`Next.js Image component unoptimized parameter behavior documentation`

💡 Result:

The unoptimized prop is a boolean on the Next.js Image component (imported from next/image). Default is false. - unoptimized={false} (default): Next.js optimizes the image by resizing, changing quality, converting format (e.g., to WebP/AVIF), and serving appropriately sized variants via the Image Optimization API. - unoptimized={true}: Serves the source image exactly as-is from the src URL. No changes to quality, size, or format. No optimization processing occurs. Useful for SVGs (auto-detected if src ends in .svg), GIFs, small images (<1KB), or auth-required images. Example: import Image from 'next/image'; const UnoptimizedImage = (props) => <Image {...props} unoptimized />; // Usage <UnoptimizedImage src="/my-image.svg" width={500} height={500} alt="Example" /> Global config (since Next.js 12.3.0): Set images: { unoptimized: true } in next.config.js to apply to all images. When unoptimized={true}, custom loader configs are ignored; it uses src directly. Official docs recommend for cases where optimization provides no benefit or is undesirable (e.g., vectors, animations).

Citations:

- 1: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/components/image
- 2: https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/image-optimization
- 3: https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image

**Disable optimization for proxied image URLs.**

Both locations set `unoptimized={!proxiedUrl}`, but `proxiedUrl` is always truthy when the Image component renders (lines 849 and 892 ensure this). This creates an unnecessary extra hop: the proxy response gets reoptimized instead of being served directly. Since the proxy already handles the external image, there's no benefit to running it through optimization again. Change to `unoptimized={Boolean(proxiedUrl)}` to serve the proxy response as-is.
